# KarirKalyan

> Full-stack job application tracker: Rails 8 API + Next.js 16 frontend

KarirKalyan is a web application for tracking job applications through a finite state machine (FSM)
pipeline. Built as a portfolio project by Chairul Akmal (https://chairulakmal.com).

## What it does

- Tracks job applications across 13 FSM states. The pipeline runs wishlist → draft → applied →
  phone_screen → technical → final_round → offer, ending in accepted, declined, rejected,
  ghosted, withdrawn or archived. It is not linear: ghosted, rejected and withdrawn can all
  return to applied. The transition table in api/app/lib/application_fsm.rb is the only
  authority on which moves are legal; this list is a summary and may lag it.
- Writes an immutable TimelineEntry on every status transition (full audit trail)
- Sends follow-up reminders via idempotent Solid Queue background jobs, batched into one digest
  per user per day rather than one email per application. The digest holds on weekends, Japanese
  national holidays, New Year, Golden Week and Obon, and the held reminders go out on the next
  business day: deferred, not dropped. With notifications enabled on /settings, the same daily
  batch also arrives as a Web Push notification on the installed app; a push failure retries
  without re-sending the email.
- Signs in with passkeys (WebAuthn) as well as a password: discoverable credentials make the
  ceremony usernameless, no authenticator restriction keeps third-party providers such as
  Proton Pass usable, and a verified assertion mints the same short-lived, revocable JWT as a
  password sign-in. The password form remains as the permanent fallback; passkey enrollment
  lives on /settings.
- Exports your data two ways: a CSV of your applications, and a full-account zip containing
  account.json plus every resume and cover letter you uploaded. Both are downloads from the
  dashboard; the zip is the one that can actually restore an account.
- Stores resume and cover letter as PostgreSQL bytea per application: 1 MB each, and at most 200
  applications per account, which is what bounds total storage; a rate limit cannot, since every
  window resets. Downloaded PDFs are named after the application they belong to
  (company-role-MMDD-id-kind.pdf), by one method shared with the archive, so a file has the same
  name whether it was downloaded on its own or unzipped out of the account export. Japanese and
  other non-ASCII names are preserved rather than transliterated.
- Exposes a stats dashboard via pure SQL aggregation (no N+1)
- Surfaces an Upcoming agenda on the dashboard: follow-ups, interviews, and the user's own
  residence-expiry clock, merged into one chronological read and computed fresh so it never goes stale
- Searches the list by free text over company/role/notes alongside the structured filters (company,
  job board, Japanese level, stage), all held in the URL so a filtered or searched view is shareable
- Records an optional note on any status change (interview details, a rejection's feedback, an
  offer's terms) against the timeline entry it belongs to; every write is confirmed with one toast
- Predicts which applications have been ghosted: the dashboard flags any application sitting in
  applied or phone_screen that has been silent longer than the 90th percentile of that user's own
  historical reply times for the stage, derived from the timeline audit trail with a window
  function. Below five recorded replies at a stage it falls back to a global default (21 days for
  applied, 14 for phone_screen) and labels the threshold as a default rather than a personal one.
- Models the Tokyo job market specifically: each application records the recruiter channel
  (direct / agency / referral) and which agency submitted it, and the app warns before a
  duplicate submission while an agency's candidate-ownership window (assumed 18 months) is
  still open; compensation is stored as the nenshu (年収) structure, guaranteed months vs
  performance-tied bonus months, rather than one number; the posting's Japanese-language
  requirement (JLPT N1/N2, business, conversational, none) is a filter; each application also
  records whether the employer sponsors a work visa (defaulting to unknown, itself a
  decision-relevant risk flag for a foreign engineer) and which status of residence a sponsored
  role falls under; it records how the company can actually employ a Japan resident (own Japan
  entity, employer-of-record, contractor-only, or cannot hire in Japan), the filter that
  silently kills most global-remote applications from Japan; and the posting text is snapshotted
  at pre-fill time so it survives the posting being taken down. These fields, sponsorship and
  hiring entity included, are extracted by the same AI pre-fill pass that reads company and
  role. The one exception is the status of residence, a manual one-tap field, since postings
  rarely name the exact status.
- Answers "is this remote role survivable from JST?": stores the company's home timezone and the
  daily overlap it requires, maps the workday into the user's clock, flags an antisocial (1am)
  start before applying, exports a timezone-correct .ics for a scheduled interview, and pushes a
  reminder the morning before it. Tracks the user's own status of residence and its expiry
  (days-remaining warning, plus the Certificate-of-Eligibility lead time a job change needs,
  sourced to current MOJ processing statistics), and a residence-expiry push as the countdown
  crosses thresholds.
- Publishes a public, no-auth Highly Skilled Professional (高度専門職) points calculator at
  /hsp-calculator: estimates the visa score on the engineering track from the MOJ points table
  (70-point threshold, J-Skip gate), unit-tested pure logic, with links out to the primary sources.
- Turns the dashboard and board into a career read over the same audit trail: response rate, ghost
  rate, and average time-in-stage stat cards; disjunctively-faceted filters whose state lives in the
  URL (a filtered view is linkable and reload-survivable); triage cards on the two candidate-side
  board columns (notes excerpt, source, days-in-stage, stalest-first); and cover-letter talking
  points extracted as bullets from the resume against the posting (bullets, not a draft).
- Offers a Kanban board at /board: one column per active status, and dragging a card between
  columns performs a real FSM transition. The board fetches the legal moves and its column set
  from GET /api/v1/transitions rather than keeping its own copy of the table, and the server
  re-validates every move regardless. Each card also has a menu listing every legal next state,
  which is the keyboard-accessible path.
- Installs as an Android PWA and registers as a share target: share a job posting from any app
  and land in the new-application form with the AI pre-fill already reading it; a share with no
  link in it seeds a paste box instead, and the same paste box is offered when a site refuses
  the automated fetch. Install goes through Chrome: share-sheet integration lives in the
  WebAPK, which Brave cannot mint. Once installed it behaves like an app: phone widths get a
  bottom tab bar, long-pressing the launcher icon offers New application and Board shortcuts,
  and a monochrome manifest icon lets Android themed icons tint the monogram.
- Cursor-based pagination on the applications list

## Registration is closed

There is no sign-up form and no sign-up endpoint. The app holds resumes (the most PII-dense
document most people own) and it is deliberately not built to be anyone's custodian but its
author's. Visitors sign in to the shared demo account below, which is the full app with twelve
applications already in it, not a stripped sandbox. Sign-in supports passkeys (WebAuthn)
alongside the password form; the demo account uses the password. Accounts are created by the
operator with `bin/rails users:create`. Data and erasure requests go to
karirkalyan@cypherpunkzero.com; there is no self-service delete button, and the app does not
claim one.

## Tech stack

- Backend: Rails 8 API-only, Ruby 3.4.9, PostgreSQL 18, Devise + devise-jwt
- Jobs & cache: Solid Queue + Solid Cache, both Postgres-backed; no Redis, no Sidekiq.
  Workers run inside Puma, so there is no separate worker service.
- Frontend: Next.js 16 App Router, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS
- Auth: JWT issued by Rails, stored in httpOnly cookie by Next.js; never reaches client JS
- Deploy: Railway (API service + web service + managed PostgreSQL)
- Tests: RSpec (unit + request specs against real DB), Vitest (pure client logic, no DOM),
  Playwright E2E, and a CI script that fails the build when the en and ja message catalogs
  disagree about which keys exist

## Live

- App: https://kk.chairulakmal.com
- Architecture, and the reasoning behind it: https://kk.chairulakmal.com/about
- API overview: https://kk.chairulakmal.com/docs
- API reference (Swagger UI): https://api-production-4899.up.railway.app/api-docs
- Japanese: the same pages under /ja (e.g. https://kk.chairulakmal.com/ja/about)
- Demo account: demo@karirkalyan.com / oretachinomachida (12 pre-loaded Tokyo tech applications)
- Privacy policy: https://kk.chairulakmal.com/privacy
- Terms: https://kk.chairulakmal.com/terms
